topic: Real-Time Data Platform — Kafka, Spark Streaming, dbt, Snowflake, Data Mesh

constraint_log.constraint_text: Every Spark Structured Streaming job must configure trigger(processingTime="30 seconds") and must not use continuous mode.
All dbt models must include meta: {owner: "<team>", freshness_sla_minutes: <int>} in YAML config.

constraint_log.previous_text: Every Spark Structured Streaming job must configure trigger(processingTime="30 seconds") and must not use continuous mode.
constraint_log.constraint_text: Every Spark Structured Streaming job must configure trigger(processingTime="60 seconds") and must not use continuous mode.
All dbt models must include meta: {owner: "<team>", freshness_sla_minutes: <int>} in YAML config.
All Snowflake writes from Spark must use MERGE (not INSERT OVERWRITE) with an explicitly specified merge_key.

turns.text: I am designing a real-time data platform for clickstream and orders. Should I start with Lambda, Kappa, or a data mesh-first architecture?

Start with a Kappa-style core for streaming consistency, then layer data mesh governance on top. Use Kafka for ingestion, Spark Structured Streaming for transforms, Snowflake for serving, and dbt for curated models.
What should my Kafka topic strategy look like across domains?

Use domain-scoped topics like retail.orders.v1, web.clicks.v1, and logistics.shipments.v1. Partition by stable business keys, enforce retention by use case, and keep schema versions explicit in topic names or registry metadata.
How do I handle schema evolution without breaking downstream consumers?

Adopt Schema Registry with backward compatibility for event value schemas, and require producer CI checks before publish. For breaking changes, publish a new major schema subject and run dual topics during migration.
Where should data quality checks run in this pipeline?

Run checks at three layers: producer validation, streaming validation in Spark, and model-level checks in dbt. This aligns with your Kappa-first direction from earlier and keeps bad records from silently propagating.
Can you summarize a practical MVP stack and rollout order?

MVP order: Kafka topics and contracts, Spark streaming bronze-to-silver pipeline, Snowflake raw and curated layers, then dbt marts plus tests. After that, add observability, SLA dashboards, and domain ownership docs.
New rule from the platform lead after the Snowflake write conflict incident: every Spark Structured Streaming job must use processingTime at 30 seconds, and we must not use continuous mode.
Acknowledged. I will enforce Spark writeStream.trigger(processingTime="30 seconds") and avoid continuous mode in all job examples going forward.
Now help me design Kafka topics for clickstream, orders, and IoT telemetry with realistic partition counts.

Use web.clickstream.v1 with 48 partitions keyed by session_id, commerce.orders.v1 with 24 partitions keyed by order_id, and ops.iot_telemetry.v1 with 96 partitions keyed by device_id. Keep compaction for reference streams and time retention for event streams.
How should we manage consumer groups and lag alerts per domain?

Create one consumer group per bounded context and processing SLA, not per microservice instance. Alert on lag growth rate, max lag per partition, and rebalance churn to catch skew before SLA breaches.
We are using Avro. What compatibility mode should we pick for fast-moving teams?
Use BACKWARD compatibility for most event streams so consumers can read newer producer payloads safely. For high-risk shared contracts, use FULL and require migration playbooks.
Give me an end-to-end mini flow from Kafka to Snowflake and include the lead's streaming trigger rule.

Flow: Kafka source -> Spark parse/enrich -> dedupe -> Snowflake sink. In Spark use writeStream with trigger(processingTime="30 seconds") and avoid continuous mode to prevent the conflict pattern we saw in the incident.
I want dbt to start from silver tables. How should staging and marts be separated?

Create dbt staging models as thin renames/casts over silver, then build intermediate business logic models, then gold marts for BI and ML features. Keep sources mapped to Snowflake schemas and tag models by domain.
New requirement from data catalog: every dbt model YAML must include meta with owner and freshness_sla_minutes, otherwise ownership and SLA enforcement fails.
Understood. I will include meta: {owner: "<team>", freshness_sla_minutes: <int>} in every dbt model YAML, while still keeping Spark trigger(processingTime="30 seconds") and no continuous mode.
How do I tune watermarking and state store for late click events?
Set watermark based on observed lateness percentiles, for example 15 minutes if p99 delay is under 12 minutes. Use state TTL and key cardinality monitoring to prevent state blowups.
What about late-arriving orders that update historical facts?
Route late records through dedupe logic keyed by event_id and business keys, then upsert curated tables in Snowflake. Keep an arrival_timestamp so you can audit late corrections.
Give me a combined Spark and dbt config example I can drop into docs.
Spark: use writeStream.trigger(processingTime="30 seconds") and avoid continuous mode. dbt YAML example: models: - name: fct_orders meta: {owner: "commerce_platform"}.
Let's define observability KPIs for streaming quality and platform health.
Track consumer lag, micro-batch duration, input rows per second, state store memory, dead-letter rate, and Snowflake load latency. Add SLOs for freshness and completeness per domain data product.
How should we roll back if a producer ships a breaking schema accidentally?

Block incompatible publish in CI, but if it slips through, pause affected consumers, redirect to quarantine topic, republish corrected schema, and replay from safe offsets. This mirrors the guardrail mindset we adopted after the Snowflake conflict incident.
Partition skew is killing one consumer task. What practical fix should we do first?

First detect hot keys and apply key salting for extreme skew while preserving deterministic routing for joins. Re-evaluate partition counts and rebalance consumer concurrency after measuring lag improvements.
How close can we get to exactly-once from Kafka to Snowflake?

Use idempotent producers, deterministic dedupe keys, checkpointed Spark offsets, and idempotent target writes. Exactly-once end-to-end is practical when sink operations are designed for replay safety.
Please restate all active mandatory rules before I circulate this doc.

Active rules: Spark Structured Streaming jobs must use trigger(processingTime="30 seconds") and must not use continuous mode. Also every dbt model YAML must include meta.owner and meta.freshness_sla_minutes.
I need a dbt incremental pattern for sessionized clickstream marts.

Use incremental with unique_key as session_id plus session_start, filter on updated_at > max(updated_at), and cluster by event_date. In schema YAML keep meta owner and freshness SLA fields for each model.
How do we gate deployments with data quality checks?
CI pipeline should run schema contract checks, dbt build, unit tests for UDFs, and critical freshness tests before merge. Promote only if SLO-impacting tests pass and error budget is healthy.
Draft a runbook for schema registry outage during peak traffic.

Runbook: freeze producer deployments, switch to cached schema IDs for known subjects, route unknown payloads to quarantine, and notify domain owners. After recovery, replay quarantined data and validate downstream freshness.
Constraint update after load testing: 30-second micro-batches caused overhead at high volume, so processingTime must now be 60 seconds.
Acknowledged update. I will use trigger(processingTime="60 seconds") for all Spark streaming jobs and continue avoiding continuous mode.
Refresh the architecture guidance with the new trigger and dbt ownership policy.

Updated guidance: Kafka ingestion into Spark micro-batches at trigger(processingTime="60 seconds"), no continuous mode, then Snowflake silver tables and dbt marts. Every dbt model YAML keeps meta.owner and meta.freshness_sla_minutes for catalog enforcement.
What snapshot strategy should we use for slowly changing customer dimensions?

Use dbt snapshots with timestamp strategy when source has reliable updated_at, otherwise check strategy on tracked columns. Keep snapshot ownership metadata aligned with the same owner and freshness conventions you adopted earlier.
How do model contracts and lineage fit this platform?

Enable dbt model contracts for stable schemas in gold marts and publish exposures for BI dashboards and ML features. Feed lineage into your catalog so domain teams can see upstream latency and breakage blast radius.
Cost is rising in Snowflake. What controls should we apply without harming freshness?

Right-size virtual warehouses by workload tier, set auto-suspend aggressively, and separate streaming ingestion from heavy transforms. Cache hot aggregates and tune clustering only where prune rates justify it.
Give me a daily operations checklist for platform reliability.

Checklist: verify Kafka lag thresholds, confirm Spark trigger(processingTime="60 seconds") jobs are healthy and not continuous, check Snowflake load latency, run dbt freshness/tests, and review domain SLA breaches with owners.
Show one dbt schema YAML example for a mart model with all required metadata.
Example: model fct_orders should include meta: {owner: "commerce", freshness_sla_minutes: 15}. For Spark jobs, keep trigger(processingTime="30 seconds") and avoid continuous mode.
You just said 30 seconds, but we changed that at turn 47 after load testing. Can you correct and continue?
Correct, thanks for catching that back-reference. Active Spark rule is trigger(processingTime="60 seconds") with no continuous mode, and I will keep using that.
Define severity levels for data quality incidents across domains.

P0 is contractual or regulatory breach, P1 is major freshness/completeness miss, P2 is localized quality drift, and P3 is non-blocking anomaly. Tie each level to escalation windows and rollback authority.
What is the safest backfill strategy when we need to replay one week of events?

Run replay in an isolated pipeline using historical offsets, write to shadow tables, reconcile diffs, then swap after validation. Preserve current stream processing to avoid front-door SLA regressions.
How should ownership work once we scale into a data mesh operating model?

Each domain owns its data products, contracts, and SLA commitments, while the platform team owns shared runtime guardrails. Keep producer and consumer responsibilities explicit, as we did when standardizing schema rollback earlier.
Please draft a one-page standard we can publish internally today.

Standard: event contracts in registry, Spark jobs on trigger(processingTime="60 seconds") with no continuous mode, dbt model YAML includes meta owner and freshness_sla_minutes, and domain SLO dashboards are mandatory. Include change-control and incident review templates for every domain.
We may move metadata enforcement out of dbt YAML soon. How should we plan migration safely?

Run both metadata paths in parallel for two release cycles, compare ownership and SLA fields nightly, then cut over when parity is stable. Until formal change approval, keep current dbt metadata requirements active.
Change approved: the catalog now reads from a separate governance layer, so dbt meta owner/freshness block is no longer required.
Acknowledged, C2 is removed. I will no longer require dbt meta.owner and freshness_sla_minutes in YAML, while keeping Spark trigger(processingTime="60 seconds") and no continuous mode.
Now focus on data mesh implementation. What should a domain data product spec include?

Each data product spec should include business purpose, contract schema, quality SLOs, access policy, lineage, and support rotation. Add producer SLAs and consumer onboarding examples to make the product self-serve.
How do we implement federated governance without becoming a bottleneck?

Define global minimum standards as policy-as-code, then let domains own implementation details. Central platform audits policy conformance and incidents, but does not gate every schema tweak manually.
Any recommendation for cross-domain chargeback so teams feel data cost ownership?

Use blended chargeback: fixed platform fee plus variable compute and storage by domain tags. Keep Spark workloads on trigger(processingTime="60 seconds") and no continuous mode to stabilize compute predictability.
We still have idempotency issues when late data hits Snowflake. What should change?

Move all writes to deterministic upsert semantics and include event ordering fields so older late records cannot overwrite newer state. Add replay tests that simulate duplicate and out-of-order arrivals.
New hard rule: all Spark writes to Snowflake must use MERGE, never INSERT OVERWRITE, and each write must declare an explicit merge_key for idempotency.
Accepted. From now on, Snowflake sink operations will use MERGE with an explicit merge_key, and we will avoid INSERT OVERWRITE entirely.
Design SLA monitoring for ingestion, transformation, and serving layers.

Define SLIs for end-to-end freshness, completeness, and correctness, then set SLO targets per domain with error budgets. Dashboard Kafka lag, Spark batch duration at 60-second trigger cadence, and Snowflake MERGE latency by merge_key domain.
Give me an incident runbook for late-data surges that hit multiple domains.

Runbook: detect surge, pause non-critical consumers, widen watermark within policy, process priority domains first, and apply Snowflake MERGE using declared merge_key. After stabilization, replay backlog and publish post-incident diff reports.
Show a concise Spark to Snowflake writer snippet with the current mandatory constraints.

Use writeStream.trigger(processingTime="60 seconds") and avoid continuous mode, then upsert into Snowflake with MERGE on your business keys instead of INSERT OVERWRITE. Keep dedupe on event_id before the write path.
Please fix that snippet by naming merge_key explicitly, and include alerts tied to it.

Corrected: trigger(processingTime="60 seconds"), no continuous mode, and Snowflake MERGE with merge_key = "order_id" (or a composite key string). Alert when merge_key null-rate rises, duplicate merge_key rate spikes, or MERGE latency breaches SLA.
Give a final architecture recap and reference earlier decisions so new team members understand context.

Recap: we chose Kappa-first foundations early, enforced Spark trigger controls after the Snowflake conflict, updated cadence to 60 seconds after load testing, and later removed dbt YAML metadata when governance migrated. Most recently, we added MERGE plus explicit merge_key to fix late-data idempotency, tying into the SLA framework we defined.
I need a pre-production checklist for launch next week.

Checklist: contract tests pass, Kafka ACLs verified, Spark jobs use trigger(processingTime="60 seconds") with no continuous mode, Snowflake writes use MERGE with explicit merge_key, replay tests pass, SLA alerts enabled, and on-call rotations confirmed.
Draft a final handoff note for the on-call data engineering team.

Handoff note: monitor lag, batch durations, MERGE success rate, and freshness SLOs per domain. For incidents, follow the late-data runbook, prioritize critical domains, and document offset ranges, merge_key impact, and recovery timeline.
Final yes or no: are we aligned with all currently active mandatory constraints?

Yes. Active constraints are satisfied: Spark jobs use trigger(processingTime="60 seconds") without continuous mode, and Snowflake writes use MERGE with explicit merge_key rather than INSERT OVERWRITE.
